coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional crosslinked chitosan/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot for wastewater treatment
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 THE AUTHORS. Published by Elsevier BV on behalf of Faculty of Engineering, Ain Shams University.
coredata.prism:publisher: THE AUTHORS. Published by Elsevier BV on behalf of Faculty of Engineering, Ain Shams University.
coredata.dc:creator: Amari, Abdelfattah
Elboughdiri, Noureddine
Ghernaout, Djamel
Lajimi, Ramzi Hadj
Alshahrani, Ali M.
Tahoon, Mohamed A.
Rebah, Faouzi Ben
coredata.dc:description: The fabrication of multifunctional nanomaterial for the adsorption of different types of pollutants is a challenge and could be a solution for many problems associated with wastewater treatment. This current study propose the use of crosslinked chitosan/nitrogen doped-graphene quantum dot nanocomposites (CS/NGQD) synthesized via glutaraldehyde as cross-linker to remove various pollutants including bacteria, proteins, fluoride, dyes, and pharmaceuticals. This nanomaterial was characterized using different techniques (FT-IR, XRD, SEM, TEM, and Raman spectroscopy). This nanocomposite offered higher pollutants removal efficiencies compared to its component parts. Using CS/NGQD the removal reached 60% for proteins and 88% for the bacterial load. A significant removal enhancement was also obtained for pharmaceuticals (63% for chlorothiazide, 81% for carbamazepine, and 89% ranitidine), dyes (82% for Azo blue, 84% for methylene blue and 94% for orange G), and fluoride (42%). Interestingly, after 5 cycles of reuse, the nanocomposite remains efficient for the removal of pollutants from water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Water treatment
Nanomaterials
Quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD, (b) Raman shift, and (c) FT-IR of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot, chitosan, and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dot/chitosan nanocomposite.
SEM image of (a) CS, (b) CS/NGQD and (c) TEM image of CS/NGQD.
Multiple contaminants removal efficiency using synthesized materials at 303.15 K and pH of 6; (a) bacteria and protein, (b) pharmaceuticals, and (c) fluoride and dyes.

Reusability of CS/NGQD nanocomposite for the removal of different pollutants at 303.15 K and pH of 6 for five successive cycles; (a) bacteria and protein, (b) pharmaceuticals, and (c) fluoride and dyes.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional crosslinked chitosan/nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot for wastewater treatment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fabrication of multifunctional nanomaterial for the adsorption of different types of pollutants is a challenge and could be a solution for many problems associated with wastewater treatment. This current study propose the use of crosslinked chitosan/nitrogen doped-graphene quantum dot nanocomposites (CS/NGQD) synthesized via glutaraldehyde as cross-linker to remove various pollutants including bacteria, proteins, fluoride, dyes, and pharmaceuticals. This nanomaterial was characterized using different techniques (FT-IR, XRD, SEM, TEM, and Raman spectroscopy). This nanocomposite offered higher pollutants removal efficiencies compared to its component parts. Using CS/NGQD the removal reached 60% for proteins and 88% for the bacterial load. A significant removal enhancement was also obtained for pharmaceuticals (63% for chlorothiazide, 81% for carbamazepine, and 89% ranitidine), dyes (82% for Azo blue, 84% for methylene blue and 94% for orange G), and fluoride (42%). Interestingly, after 5 cycles of reuse, the nanocomposite remains efficient for the removal of pollutants from water.

ce:section.ce:para: Clean water is essential for a better life and the reuse of wastewater after treatment is helpful to resolve water shortage [1,2]. The adsorption process remains an interesting method which is safe, clean, efficient and useful for the removal of various pollutants. Therefore, the fabrication of new suitable material for water treatment attracted the attention of researchers [3,4]. The use of the nanomaterials as adsorbents showed higher efficiencies and fast adsorption rates of various pollutants. Generally, nanomaterials adsorption performances depend mainly on the used preparation method and the operating conditions (pH, temperature, pollutant concentrations, biosorbent dosage and size, etc) as reported by many researchers [5–12]. The used modification process may enhance nanoparticles stability and the zeta potential. Interestingly, the initial adsorbent dose is an important factor to overcome mass transfer resistance between the aqueous and solid phases. Moreover, the particle size of the materials influences the biosorption process and smaller particles offer larger surface area of the biosorbent [9,10]. Concerning the contact time, this parameter coupled to an adequate stirring rate may permit good mass transfer by reducing the boundary layer width involving the adsorbate and the adsorbent [11]. However, the pH remains the most important factors that affect the surface behavior of the adsorbent [12]. Therefore, is important to choose the useful process for the preparation nanoparticles and optimize the operating parameters for each adsorption experiment.

Today, bioactive low-cost natural polymers like chitosan (CS) attract the attention of researchers in several applications such as tissue and biomaterials engineering due to their large surface area, biodegradability, biocompatibility, and non-toxicity. CS is an exceptional amino-polysaccharide obtained via treatment with strong alkaline medium. Crustaceans, insect exoskeletons, and fungal cell walls are the main sources of chitosan natural polymers [13–16]. However, CS applications are limited due to some problems such as surface roughness, aqueous uncontrolled swelling, fast degradation rate, and low mechanical properties. To overcome these limitations chitosan polymers can be incorporated with nano-fillers such as graphene quantum dot (GQD) or cross-links via ionic and covalent crosslinking [17]. NH2 groups of Cs bonded the cross-linker such as glutaraldehyde (GA) to synthesize 3D structure with improved swelling features, mechanical properties, and stability [18]. GQD is carbonaceous zero-dimensional nanomaterial results from graphene and carbon dots with a width less than 20 nm. GQD is a promising material for different applications like antimicrobial materials, drug delivery, biosensors, and bio-imaging due to their exceptional properties like biocompatibility, brilliant bioactivity, and high solubility in several solvents, low toxicity, noticed photostability, and high hydrophilicity [18,19]. Also, they are applied as absorbent for wastewater treatment offering a high efficiency [20] due to their edge effects and quantum confinement. More active sites for GQD can be provided via doping with heteroatom nitrogen or sulfur element [21]. Thus, the polymeric composite can be richening with high number of binding sites via combination with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (NGQDs). The doping process provides an active chelating site by doing as a dynamic electron donor. The doping process improves the adsorption performance toward different contaminants via several interactions such as electrostatic and hydrophobic interactions, π– π stacking, and hydrogen bonding. So, N-free GQDs have less performance than NGQDs in pollutant removal. The solubility problems prevent the use of NGQDs alone for water treatment. So, it is incorporated with a polymeric matrix for better physicochemical characters and higher removal capacity of contaminants. This coupling of CS and NGQDs provides a multifunctional nanomaterial with exceptional properties for the removal of several contaminants such as bacteria, pharmaceutical compounds, fluoride, and dyes from wastewaters. Thus, the novelty of this work is the fabrication of a new nanocomposite with a very simple and easy method for the elimination of several types of contaminants even bacteria.
Acros (New Jersey, USA) is the source of the used chitosan in this study with a molecular weight of 600–800 × 103 g/mol. All other used chemicals were supplied from Merck Co., Germany.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (NGQD)
ce:section.ce:para: NGQD was synthesized according to the modified method of Achadu and Nyokong [22] by Qu et al. [23] in which the synthesis occurred via the hydrothermal treatment of carbon source (citric acid) in the existence of nitrogen source (urea). 6 mL of distilled water was used to dissolve 3 mol of urea and 1 mol of citric acid that shacked until clear then heated for 7 h at 185 °C in Teflon tied stainless autoclave. The produced dark powder of NGQD was then extracted by the addition of ethyl alcohol. Then the powder was centrifuged and dried at the normal temperature.

The solution casting method under sonochemistry was used for the synthesis of CCS/NGQD nanocomposite in which 8 mL of 1% citric acid was used to dissolve of 0.1 g chitosan then ultra-sonicated for 6 min. After that, NGQD (5 wt%) previously dissolved in water was added slowly with vigorous stirring for five hours to chitosan solution. The obtained mixture was ultra-sonicated for half-hour followed by the addition of (GA, 10 wt%) with vigorous stirring up to 24 h. The produced nanocomposite was dried and sinking for two hours in (NaOH, 2 wt%) to neutralize the dark brown nanocomposite [24].

The synthesized nanomaterials were characterized using different techniques including FT-IR, XRD, SEM, TEM, and Raman spectroscopy. FT-IR of samples was performed using Perkin Elmer Spectrum two spectrometers (United States). XRD was performed using Rigaku Ultima Diffractometer 1840 (Japan). Raman spectroscopic analysis was performed using a T64000 Raman spectroscope. SEM analysis was performed using HITACHI S-4160 while TEM analysis was performed using Philips CM 120.

The prepared nanomaterials were tested for the removal of different pollutants. The examined pollutants included E. coli as an example of bacteria, protein as an example of biomolecules, pharmaceuticals (chlorothiazide, carbamazepine, and ranitidine), dyes (orange G, methylene blue, azo blue), and fluoride ions. The ability of the synthesized nanomaterials to remove proteins from the water was studied using bovine serum albumin that can cause many problems when released in wastewater while E. coli was used as an indication of the synthesized nanomaterials ability to remove dangerous bacteria.

Fluoride ions and previously mentioned dyes were removed by adding 0.12 g of synthesized nanomaterials into pollutant aqueous solution (11 mg/L for fluoride and dyes, 100 mL) under continuous shaking (150 rpm) for 185 min at 303.15 K and pH of 6 maintained using standard solutions of 0.1 N sodium hydroxide and 0.1 N hydrochloric acid. Fluoride ions and dyes removal efficiency was calculated using Eq. (1). The dye concentrations before and after adsorption were analyzed using a UV/VIS spectrophotometer at 476 nm, 620 nm and 665 nm for orange G, methylene blue, and azo blue, respectively. Fluoride was determined using the standard addition method with an F ion meter (PXSJ-216 F, Shanghai INESA Scientific Instrument Ltd., China). The pharmaceuticals removal efficiency was calculated using the same equation (Eq. (1)) using the same conditions applied for dyes and fluoride removals, except the pharmaceutical concentration was 50 mg/L. The concentrations of chlorothiazide, carbamazepine and ranitidine were measured using a UV/VIS spectrophotometer at 293 nm, 286 nm, and 313 nm, respectively.
(1)
R E
( % )
=
C i
-
C t
)
C i
× 100
where Ci denotes the initial pollutant concentration and Ct denotes its concentration after the experimental time.

For the bacterial removal, an adsorbent dose of 0.42 g was used with an initial bacterial load of 7 × 106 cfu/mL of water. The mixture was incubated under shaking at pH 6 and 303.15 K for 185 min. Spread plate counting and standard serial dilution methods were used for E. coli load determination in the solution. Bacterial removal efficiency (RE) was determined according to Eq. (2).
(2)
R E
%
=
N i
-
N t
)
N i
× 100
where Ni denotes the initial E. coli load and Nt denotes its load after the mentioned experimental time.

For the protein removal the same conditions for bacterial removal were used with (500 mg/L of bovine serum albumin, 100 mL). The bovine serum albumin removal efficiency (RE) was calculated using Eq. (3).
(3)
R E
( % )
=
C i
-
C t
)
C i
× 100
where Ci denotes the bovine serum albumin initial concentration and Ct denotes its concentration after the experimental time.

The XRD of CS, NGQD, and CS/ NGQD is shown in Fig. 1 a. According to XRD of NGQD, there is a broad peak at 2theta = 22° representing the hexagonal graphite plane of (002) and may be belonging to the NGQD surface defects or reduced oxygen produced via doping process. The total carbonization of citric acid to NGQD was indicated from the absence of citric acids peaks. The crystallinity of combined CS decreases than pure one at 19.8° due to the cross-linking with glutaraldehyde that destroyed the regularity of pure CS via breaking the intra-molecular and inter-molecular hydrogen bonds as reported in the literature [25]. But, herein the combined CS crystallinity is more decreased than expected that may related to glutaraldehyde low concentration. As shown in the XRD pattern the addition of quantum dots to CS led to the existence of the same peaks like CS with a small shift of characteristic peak to an upper angle equal to 20.8° that related to electrostatic attractions and H-bonds between CS chains and doped quantum dots. Raman shift was used to explore the combination between CS and NGQD as shown in Fig. 1b. According to the spectra, there is two characteristic wide bands of NGQD at 1370 and 1590 cm−1. The two bands are corresponding to breathing mode from sp3 defects and E2g mode from the vibration of bonded sp2 carbons, respectively. The redshift of the first peak to lower value 1360 cm−1 was observed after the addition of NGQD over CS polymer as well as it becomes more eminent. The ratio of two characteristic bands intensities was used to assess the structural defects. This ratio was found equal to 0.74 and 0.62 for CS/NGQD and NGQD, respectively. As noticed, this ratio increased after interaction between CS and NGQD due to H-bonds between two materials and also, a decrease in the number of oxide functional groups resulted from nucleophilic substitution between NGQD and CS sites.

FT-IR spectra of CS, NGQD, and CS/NGQD are shown in Fig. 1c. According to the spectrum of CS, bands at 1384, 1422, and 1599 cm−1 are corresponding to CH symmetrical bending vibration, CH2 bending, and NH bending, respectively while CH stretching vibration, OH and NH2 stretching vibrations are represented by 2920, 2861, and 3421 cm−1 bands, respectively. The band at 1654 cm−1 represents CO stretching of amide. The cross-linking reaction between CS and aldehyde of glutaraldehyde produced an amine group represented by a peak at 1641 cm−1 corresponding to CN bond vibrations. Also, there is no band at 1720 cm−1 that belongs to free aldehyde due to the low glutaraldehyde concentration in chitosan. According to the spectrum of NGQD, CO and CN bond stretching vibrations are represented by bands at 1651 and 1418 cm−1, respectively. COO– occurring on the boundaries of the NGQDs asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations is represented by peaks at 1385 and 1573 cm−1. Peaks at 2858 and 2931 cm−1 represent asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the CH bond. OH– and NH– stretching vibrations are represented by the wideband at 3100–3500 cm−1. FT-IR spectrum of NGQD reveals no characteristic bands of the aromatic ring. According to the FT-IR spectrum of CS/ NGQD nanocomposite, all bands of NGQD and CS were represented with a small difference in the intensity. Additionally, there is a redshift of bands at 1599 and 3453 cm−1 corresponding to NH2 and OH groups to 1597 and 3429 cm−1. These deviations were due to the CS and NGQD functional groups combination via hydrogen bonding. Additionally, SEM and TEM images of synthesized materials were performed and shown in Fig. 2 for more characterization. According to the SEM images of CS and CS/NGQD nanocomposite (Fig. 2a and b), there is a clear difference in the surface morphology before and after loading the NGQD to CS. Without NGQD, CS has a rubble-like structure with homogenous and compact surface as shown in Fig. 2a. SEM image of CS/NGQD nanocomposite has small ruptures and cracks on the surface providing a higher effective area for contaminants capturing. The TEM image of CS/NGQD nanocomposite (Fig. 2c) shows a well-distributed quantum dots over the chitosan matrix.

Protein removal efficiency using synthesized nanomaterials presented in Fig. 3 a indicated that CS removed 16% of protein while NGQD removed 45%. After the accumulation of doped quantum dot over polymer matrix 60% of the protein was removed indicating an enhanced efficiency. Additionally, the CS/NGQD nanocomposite showed enhanced bacterial removal than CS and NGQD. The nanocomposite removed 88% of the initial bacterial load as shown in Fig. 3a while CS only removed 19% and NGQD removed 80% of the initial bacterial load. Another type of contaminants that must be controlled powerfully is pharmaceuticals. The synthesized nanomaterials were tested here for the removal of three drugs (chlorothiazide, carbamazepine, and ranitidine). As shown in Fig. 3b, the three drugs can be removed by the CS, NGQD, and CS/NGQD nanocomposite. But, the CS/NGQD nanocomposite showed higher abilities for drug removal than uncombined counterparts. The obtained values were 89%, 81%, 63% for ranitidine, carbamazepine and chlorothiazide, respectively. The better removal efficiency of CS/NGQD is due to the improvement of the surface properties by loading the doped quantum dots over chitosan support.

Moreover, the synthesized nanomaterials were tested for the removal of many dyes and fluoride as shown in Fig. 3c. After the adsorption experiment, the residual dyes and fluoride were monitored to get the removal efficiency. As shown in Fig. 3c, the CS/NGQD allowed better removal efficiency for all dyes (82–94%) and fluoride (42%) than CS (the highest value of 37% was obtained for orange G) and NGQD (the highest value of 50% was obtained for orange G). As indicated above, this fact is due to the improvement of the surface properties by loading the doped quantum dots over chitosan support. Compared to other studies, the prepared CS/NGQD nanocomposite displayed accepted removal rates of various pollutants. Generally, the obtained efficiency removal of CS/NGQD varied from other nanomaterials (hausmannite, manganese oxide nanostructures, modified zeolite, reduced graphene oxide loaded silver, etc.) [26–29]. The high removal efficiency of nanocomposite is related to higher active site to chelate pollutants as well as the improved swelling properties that efficiently affects the surface area of nanomaterial. The adsorption process of contaminants usually depends on many factors including adsorption conditions (the pollutant dose, the adsorbent dose, the contact time, pH, temperature, etc.), polymeric structure and physical form, electronegativity, ionic radius, absolute hardness, and polarizability that derive the affinity of adsorbent to the contaminant. In the current adsorption, the π- π interactions and H-bonding could be involved in the removal process than electrostatic forces. At the studied pH value (pH = 6), OH groups are polarized and the carboxyl groups are dissociated to H+ and COO−. Therefore, in this case, there is a distribution of active sites over the nanocomposite surface represented by OH at the C3 position of chitosan, CN, and unsubstituted amino groups as well as carboxylic groups over the doped quantum dots. The interactions between nanocomposites and contaminants in this case can be occurred mainly by hydrogen bonding or electrostatic interactions or coordination via forming complexes. The present functional sites that abundant on the nanocomposite surface allowed neighboring nitrogen and oxygen ion pairs reachable by the water contaminants. The results obtained in this study are challenged with the other prepared materials for the water/wastewater treatment especially it is a multifunctional nanocomposite used for the removal of different types of pollutants. The current nanocomposite is better than materials containing graphene even provides higher results due to non-expensive and easier fabrication techniques. Generally, methods used to prepare graphene and its derivatives are expensive and necessitate advanced operational arrangement. Thus, quantum dots are cheaper than graphene oxide and graphene as well as their different properties. Thus, the current nanocomposite provides high efficiency with a cost-effective and easier preparation method. However, additional details are required such as the study of the adsorption thermodynamic parameters [30–35] and adsorption isotherm to enhance the application of CS/NGQD nanocomposite for wastewater treatment at large-scale. The results in the current study indicate that this fabricated nanocomposite is promising material for water treatment.

The main factor that determines the economic value of any adsorbent is its ability of regeneration and reusability. The recyclability of nanocomposite is an important significant characteristic for an optimal wastewater treatment. Hence, the reusability of CS/NGQD nanocomposite for the removal of the studied pollutants was tested for five successive cycles (Fig. 4 ). Each cycle was studied in triplicate.

During the reusability study for the removal of E. coli, the nano-adsorbent was washed before each cycle with 70% ethyl alcohol/H2O due to the ability of ethanol to destruct bacterial cell wall and detaches them from the surface of the adsorbent. According to Fig. 4a, the nanocomposite still captured 88% of bacterial load after two treatment cycles. Slight decrease was observed in the third cycle (86%) and in the fourth cycle (84%). In the fifth cycle, the bacterial removal dropped to 83%. For protein, before each cycle, the nanocomposite was immersed in deionized water for 30 min to detach the adsorbed protein from the adsorbent surface. Reusability data showed that the nanocomposite showed no decrease in its efficiency after two cycles (60%). However, the removal efficiency decreased to 57% and to 53% respectively after the fourth and the fifth cycles (Fig. 4a). During the reusability study toward the removal of pharmaceuticals, the nanocomposite was washed using 0.4 M NaOH followed by distilled water, acetone and methanol/NaOH (20% w/v) respectively for ranitidine, carbamazepine and chlorothiazide. According to Fig. 4b, an acceptable reusability was observed for the drugs (ranitidine, carbamazepine, and chlorothiazide) with slight removal reduction after each cycle. After five cycles, the removal efficiencies remain higher that 50% for all drugs. Also, the synthesized nanomaterial CS/NGQD was tested for reusability toward the removal of dyes and fluoride ion as shown in Fig. 4c. Before each studied cycle, the adsorbent was washed several times with 0.1 N HCl and NaOH for the desorption process. Interestingly, in the case of dyes (azo blue, methylene blue, orange G) and fluoride there was no drastic effect on the removal efficiency after five cycles of reusability of the nanocomposite CS/NGQD. After five cycles, the removal efficiencies were 74%, 73%, 84% and 34% respectively for azo blue, methylene blue, orange G and fluoride.

This work indicates that the synthesized CS/NGQD nanocomposite offered higher pollutants removal efficiencies compared to its component parts; CS and NGQD. This nanocomposite allowed the removal of 60% for protein and 88% for the bacterial load. Higher removal efficiencies were also observed for pharmaceuticals (63% for chlorothiazide, 81% for carbamazepine, and 89% ranitidine), dyes (82% for Azo blue, 84% for methylene blue and 94% for orange G), and fluoride (42%). Interestingly, the reusability study of CS/NGQD nanocomposite were performed up to five cycles indicating that the removal rates of protein, bacteria, pharmaceuticals, dyes and fluoride remain at acceptable levels without significant decrease of the nanocomposite efficiency. Therefore, the ability of the synthesized CS/NGQD to remove different types of contaminants with high efficiency makes it useful for industrial wastewater treatment. Additionally, its cost-effective properties and exceptional superiority enhance its application as adsorbent of hazardous pollutants from wastewater. Nevertheless, the ability to use this nanocomposite for wastewater treatment at large scale still required more investigations.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research has been funded by the Research Deanship of University of Ha’il, Saudi Arabia, through the Project RG-20 113.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Agro-industrial waste materials and wastewater as growth media for microbial bioflocculants production: a review
Mater Res Express

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effective heavy metals removal from water using nanomaterials: a review
Processes

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Removal of meloxicam, piroxicam and Cd+2 by Fe3O4/SiO2/glycidyl methacrylate-S-SH nanocomposite loaded with laccase
Alexandria Eng J

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of synthesized nanoparticles and henna-tragacanth solutions on oil/water interfacial tension: nanofluids stability considerations
Petroleum

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Tavakoli R, Shadizadeh SR, Hayati F, Fattahi M. Effect of Synthesized Nanoparticles and Henna-Tragacanth Solutions on Oil/Water Interfacial Tension: Nanofluids Stability Considerations. Petroleum 2020. doi: 10.1016/j.petlm.2020.03.001.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of graphene Oxide/TiO2 nanocomposite, graphene oxide nanosheets and Cedr extraction solution on IFT reduction and ultimate oil recovery from a carbonated rock
Petroleum

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Garmroudi A, Kheirollahi M, Mousavi SA, Fattahi M, Mahvelati EH. ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr Abdelfattah Amari is an Assistant Professor of Chemical Engineering at King Khalid University, Abha, KSA. He received his PhD Chemical Engineering-Processes in 2010 from National School of Engineers-Gabes- Tunisia. His research interest includes chemical reaction engineering, wastewater Treatment by physicochemical methods, treatment of liquid and gaseous effluents, the use of clay for water and air purification, etc.

Dr Mohamed A. Tahoon Holder of a Master’s degree in Physical Chemistry from Mansoura University (Egypt), Dr. Tahoon earned his Ph.D. in Physical chemistry from the same University. Now, he is working at King Khalid University. His research interest includes thermodynamics, materials synthesis and characterization, computational chemistry, electrochemistry, and nanomaterials applications.

Dr Faouzi Ben Rebah Holder of a Master’s degree in Science/Environmental Engineering from Sherbrooke University (Canada), Dr. Ben Rebah earned his PhD in Water Sciences from Quebec University (Canada). Recently, he obtained a certificate on Industrial Effluents Treatment and Residuals Management from UNESCO-IHE (The Netherlands). His research efforts are concentrated on wastewater treatment and the production of high-value-added products from waste and natural sources.
